concepts.title: Orbital Speed
concepts.conceptBody:
Planetary Motion & Satellites

Planets: Planets are large natural bodies rotating around the sun due to the gravitational attraction of the sun. Path of these planets are elliptical with the sun at one focus. However, the difference in major and minor axis is not large. The orbits can therefore, be treated as nearly circular for not 700 sophisticated calculations.

Satellites: Satellites are natural or artificial bodies moving around a planet under its gravitational attraction. Satellites are launched from the earth so as to move round it. To establish the artificial satellite in the desired orbit, a number of rockets are fired from the satellites at proper time. After placing in desired orbit, it will continue to move in that orbit under gravitational attraction of the earth. We shall here now compute and discuss some physical entities of interest for a satellite undergoing circular motion.

Orbital Speed 

Let the radius of orbit be $\text{r}$ and the speed of the satellite be $\text{v}$.

According to Newton's second law,

$\frac{\text{GMm}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}}$$~=\text{m}\left( \frac{\text{v}_{0}^{2}}{\text{r}} \right)$

Or ${{\text{v}}_{0}} ~=\sqrt{\frac{\text{GM}}{\text{r}}}$

The speed of planet is inversely proportional to the square root of the radius of its orbit.

concepts.title: Time Period of Revolution of saltellite
concepts.conceptBody:
Time Period of Revolution

The time taken by a satellite in completing one revolution is its time period $\text{T}$.

$\text{T}=\frac{2\pi \text{r}}{{{v}_{0}}}=\frac{2\pi \text{r}}{\sqrt{\frac{\text{GM}}{\text{r}}}}=2\pi \sqrt{\frac{{{\text{r}}^{3}}}{\text{GM}}}=2\pi \sqrt{\frac{\text{r}}{\text{g}}}$
If the satellite is revolving close to the surface of earth i.e. $r=R$
$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{T} & =2\pi \sqrt{\frac{\text{R}}{\text{g}}}\approx 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{6.4\times {{10}^{6}}}{10}}=2\pi \times 800\text{ s} \end{array}$
$=84.6\text{ min}\approx 1.4\text{ h}$

concepts.title: Angular Momentum
concepts.conceptBody:
Angular Momentum

In case of satellite motion angular momentum will be given by

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{L} =\text{mvr}=\text{mr}\sqrt{\frac{\text{GM}}{\text{r}}}\left[ \because v=\sqrt{\frac{\text{GM}}{\text{r}}} \right] \\  \end{array}$

$\text{i}\text{.e}\text{.}$ L $~={{\left( {{\text{m}}^{2}}\text{GMr} \right)}^{1/2}}$

Note : In case of satellite, force is central, so torque $=0$ on hence angular momentum of satellite is conserved i.e. $\text{L}=$ constant.

concepts.title: Energy in Planetary and Satellite Motion
concepts.conceptBody:
Energy in Planetary and Satellite Motion

The kinetic energy of planet is

$\text{K}=\frac{1}{2}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}{{v}^{2}}$

Or $\text{K}=\frac{1}{2}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}\frac{\text{GM}}{\text{r}}\left[ \because v=\sqrt{\frac{\text{GM}}{\text{r}}} \right]$

Or $\text{K}=\frac{\text{GMm}}{2\text{r}}$

The gravitational potential energy of the earth-satellite system is $\text{V}=-\frac{\text{GMm}}{\text{r}}$

The total energy of earth-satellite system is

E = U + K

$~=-\frac{\text{GMm}}{\text{r}}+\frac{\text{GMm}}{2\text{r}}$

$~=-\frac{\text{GMm}}{2\text{r}}$

From above discussion, we obtain

$\frac{\text{K}}{\text{E}}=-1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ K}=-\text{E}$

And $\frac{\text{U}}{\text{E}}=2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ U}=2\text{E}$

i.e., kinetic energy of a satellite in an orbit is equal to negative of total energy while potential energy is equal to twice the total energy.

concepts.title: Geostationary Satellites
concepts.conceptBody:
Geostationary Satellites

If an artificial satellite revolves around the earth in an equatorial plane with a time period of 24 $\text{h}$ in the same sense as that of earth, then it will appear stationary to the observer on earth. Such a satellite is known as geostationary satellite.

At a given place $(\text{g}=$ constant), the period of revolution of earth's satellite depends solely on its height above the surface of the earth. Let us calculate the height of the satellite above the surface of the earth, so that the satellite will have a period of revolution of $24\text{ h}$ and will appear as a satellite. The height of the satellite is given by
$\text{T}=2\pi \sqrt{\frac{{{\text{r}}^{3}}}{\text{ g}{{\text{R}}^{2}}}}=\frac{2\pi }{\text{R}}{{\left[ \frac{{{(\text{R}+\text{h})}^{3}}}{\text{ g}} \right]}^{1/2}}$
Or $\text{h}={{\left( \frac{{{\text{T}}^{2}}{{\text{R}}^{2}}\text{ g}}{4{{\pi }^{2}}} \right)}^{1/3}}-\text{R}$
Putting
T = $=24\text{ h}=243600\text{ s},$
R $=6400\text{ km}=6.4{{10}^{6}}\text{ m},\text{ g}=9.8\text{ m}{{\text{s}}^{-2}},\text{ we }\text{ find }$
h $={{\left( \frac{{{(24\times 3600)}^{2}}\times {{\left( 6.4\times {{10}^{6}} \right)}^{2}}\times 9.8}{4{{\pi }^{2}}} \right)}^{1/3}}-6.4\times {{10}^{6}}$
$=35930\text{ km}\approx 36000\text{ km}$
Thus, a satellite will appear stationary, if it around the earth from west to east in an orbit coplanar equatorial plane at a height of about $36000\text{ km}$ about surface of earth. Such an orbit is known as geostationary orbit and the revolving in this orbit is known as synchronous satellite.

Using relation ${{v}_{0}}=\sqrt{\frac{g{{R}^{2}}}{R+{h}'}}$ the orbital velocity geostationary satellite comes out to be about 3.08 $\text{km}$.

Important Features : In case of satellite motion as $\text{L}=\text{m}v\text{r}=$ constant, while according to equation for velocity $v.\propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\text{r}}}$ two results appear to be contradictory. However apparent contradiction is resolved if we keep in that $v\propto \frac{1}{\text{r}}$ holds good for different points of the same

orbit while $v\propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\text{r}}}$ for different orbits as shown in Fig. below

concepts.title: Time Period of Satellite Observed from Earth
concepts.conceptBody:
Time Period of Satellite Observed from Earth

Consider a satellite in the circular orbit with the time period ${{\text{T}}_{\text{S}}}$. The earth also rotates with the time period ${{\text{T}}_{\text{E}}}=24\text{hrs}$. If an observer on the earth sees this satellite, the angular velocity of the satellite relative to the earth will be:

\(\omega_{\mathrm{SE}}=\overline{\omega_{\mathrm{S}}}-\overline{\omega_{\mathrm{e}}}\)

Hence the time for one revolution will appear to be different from ${{\text{T}}_{\text{S}}}$ if observed from earth. This time period can be calculated as ${{\text{T}}_{\text{SE}}}$ from the above equation.

1.If satellite and earth are rotating in same direction:

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \frac{2\pi }{{{\text{T}}_{\text{SE}}}} =\left| \frac{2\pi }{{{\text{T}}_{\text{S}}}}-\frac{2\pi }{{{\text{T}}_{\text{E}}}} \right| \\ \Rightarrow {{\text{T}}_{\text{SE}}} =\frac{{{\text{T}}_{\text{S}}}{{\text{T}}_{\text{E}}}}{\left| {{\text{T}}_{\text{E}}}-{{\text{T}}_{\text{S}}} \right|} \\ \end{array}$

2.If satellite & earth are rotating in opp. direction

${{\text{T}}_{\text{SE}}}=\frac{{{\text{T}}_{\text{S}}}{{\text{T}}_{\text{E}}}}{{{\text{T}}_{\text{S}}}+{{\text{T}}_{\text{E}}}}$

concepts.title: Binding Energy
concepts.conceptBody:
Binding Energy

Total mechanical energy ( $\text{PE}+\text{KE})$ of a closed system is negative. The modulus of this total mechanical energy is known as binding energy.

Therefore total mechanical energy of the particle of mass $m$ on surface of earth (M) is

$\text{E}=\text{K}+\text{U}=\frac{-\text{GMm}}{\text{R}}$

Binding energy $=\left| \text{E} \right|=\frac{\text{GMm}}{\text{R}}$

In another way we can say that this energy binds a particle to earth i.e. after supply an amount of energy equal to binding energy, the particle will be free of earth's attraction.
